A correlate of protection would more likely be an immune component directly involved in pathogen clearance or prevention of disease progression.

323.Option: Early and sustained increase in conjunctival T cell proportions, which peak at 3 weeks p.e. and remain elevated through the convalescent phase
Evidence: The most common leucocytes detected by sampling were T cells, monocytes, and “other leucocytes”, defined as CD45+ cells negative for all other immune markers (S2 Fig). During the first 2 weeks p.e., the proportion of T cells increased slightly from baseline (57 ± 8% to 67 ± 9%, p = 0.1210), and remained stable (Fig 4B). Between weeks 3 and 4, T cells proportions fluctuated and by week 6 the proportions had decreased.

The immune response associated to bacterial load observed in the first 7 weeks was also associated with conjunctival T lymphocytes (r = 0.63, p < 0.0001) and T cell survival factors (IL-2 and IL-15) were frequently detected during this period.

Diminution of the proportion of T cells in the model infection is also consistent with observations in the active stages of trachoma in humans. It has been shown that T cells, particularly CD4+ T cells, and the Th2 response correlate with scarring trachoma both locally [37,45] and systemically [46].
323.Rationale: While T cells do increase early in the infection, their proportions decrease by week 6 and do not remain elevated through the convalescent phase. Additionally, T cells are associated with both pathogenesis and clearance of infection, but their sustained elevation is not observed in this study. The data suggest that T cells are more involved in the acute phase rather than the resolution phase, making them less likely to be a correlate of protection.

323.Option: Delayed peak in pro-inflammatory cytokines such as IL-17A and IL-1β, which coincides with the resolution of clinical signs and bacterial clearance
Evidence: In our study, the increase in IL-17A was delayed, (week 11 p.e.), along with elevated levels of other pro-inflammatory (VEGF, IL-1B, IL-15, and GM-CSF). Some anti-inflammatory cytokines (IL-10, IL-4, and IL-5) were also elevated but with a delay, suggesting that a balance between both anti- and pro-inflammatory cytokines is needed to achieve disease resolution.

A longitudinal study of a Tanzanian cohort of children identified an association between progressive trachomatous scarring and elevated levels of IL-23 which indicate the importance of a Th17 response (through the analysis of IL-23A and PDGFB) [44]. Our observations of the late increase in both IL-12/23 and IL-17A concur with these findings.
323.Rationale: The delayed peak in pro-inflammatory cytokines such as IL-17A and IL-1β coincides with the resolution phase, but pro-inflammatory cytokines are generally associated with disease pathology rather than protection. The balance between pro- and anti-inflammatory cytokines is important for resolution, but a delayed peak in pro-inflammatory cytokines alone is not a strong indicator of protection. A correlate of protection would more likely be an immune component that is associated with pathogen clearance and prevention of disease progression.

323.Option: Sustained tear IgA response that persists after bacterial clearance and coincides with the resolution phase of disease
Evidence: IgA and IgG specific to Ct elementary bodies were quantified from tears to determine the local adaptive humoral response to conjunctival Ct exposure. IgG increased comparably at 2 weeks p.e. (p = 0.021), then both IgG and IgA peaked between 4 and 6 weeks p.e. (p = 0.001 and 0.002 between weeks 0 and 6 for IgG and IgA respectively), and then decreased at 11 weeks p.e. (p = 0.0098 and 0.0039 for IgG and IgA respectively) (Fig 5B).
Ct-specific IgA increased in 10/12 animals from 2 weeks p.e., but unlike IgG, continued to rise between week 6 to week 11, although this increase was not significant (p = 0.52).
The positive correlation between clinical signs and B lymphocytes infiltrate (r coefficient = 0.52, p = 0.00032).

A study in 1977 on immunoglobulins in the tears of trachoma patients reported a lower quantity of IgA in trachoma cases compared to healthy patients (without significant differences between trachoma phases) [39]. However, Taylor et al used a Cynomolgus model of trachoma and a different Ct strain (E/Bour), found a delayed increase in tear IgA after conjunctival Ct exposure, and persistence of systemic IgG [40] which is consistent with our findings.
323.Rationale: The sustained tear IgA response persists after bacterial clearance and coincides with the resolution phase of disease. This is a strong indicator of a potential correlate of protection because IgA is a local adaptive humoral response that is directly involved in mucosal immunity. The persistence of IgA in tears after bacterial clearance suggests that it may play a role in preventing reinfection or reducing disease severity, making it a promising candidate for a correlate of protection in vaccine development.

324.Option: Using a crossover design where parasites are exposed to drug and no-drug conditions in alternating periods to control for individual parasite variability
Evidence: In the anti-Wolbachia drug screen validation, a drug challenge was carried out using doxycycline and flubentylosin. An additional wash-out group, which entailed drug treatment for 7 days followed by a 7-day washout period was included in the study to evaluate any Wolbachia recrudescence.
Quantitative PCR analysis was employed at endpoints to evaluate Wolbachia reductions in response to treatment.
324.Rationale: The study does include a wash-out period to assess recrudescence, but this is not a crossover design. A true crossover design would involve alternating drug and no-drug periods within the same parasites, which is not explicitly described in the passage. The wash-out period is used to observe recovery after drug removal, not to alternate treatments.

324.Option: Comparing Wolbachia load in treated parasites to baseline levels measured immediately after isolation from in vivo hosts
Evidence: Wolbachia yields within female B. malayi after 1, 2 or 3 weeks in LEC+LEC insert co-cultures were compared with those from parasites immediately retrieved from IL-4Rα-/-IL-5-/- mice (week 0).
Quantitative PCR analysis, as previous, was employed at endpoints to evaluate Wolbachia reductions in response to treatment.
324.Rationale: While baseline comparisons are made, the passage emphasizes the importance of concurrent controls. The study specifically mentions that vehicle-treated cultures were run simultaneously and sampled at the same time points to account for natural decline, which is a stronger design feature for attributing changes to drug exposure.

324.Option: Using a within-subjects design where each female B. malayi is exposed to multiple drug treatments in sequence to compare effects directly
Evidence: In the anti-Wolbachia drug screen validation, female B. malayi in LEC bilayers were dosed with drug at 5 μM for either 7-days or 14-days before evaluation of efficacy.
In certain cultures, drugs were removed following 7-day exposure, parasites washed, and cultures extended for a further 7-day period.
324.Rationale: The study does involve sequential drug exposure (7-day treatment followed by 7-day washout), but this is not a within-subjects design comparing multiple drugs. The within-subjects aspect is not explicitly used to compare different drug treatments but rather to assess the effect of a single drug over time and after removal.

324.Option: Including concurrent vehicle-treated control cultures sampled at identical time points to account for natural decline in Wolbachia over time
Evidence: Due to significant decline in Wolbachia content with time in culture due to loss of mf production (Fig 4), vehicle treated cultures were ran simultaneously and sampled at 7 or 14 days as matched controls (Fig 5A).
Quantitative PCR analysis, as previous, was employed at endpoints to evaluate Wolbachia reductions in response to treatment.
324.Rationale: The inclusion of concurrent vehicle-treated control cultures sampled at identical time points is the most direct design feature that allows the researchers to attribute changes in Wolbachia load to drug exposure. By comparing treated parasites to controls that experience the same natural decline over time, the study can isolate the effect of the drug from natural physiological changes.

325.Option: Reduced predation pressure in oil palm plantations due to lower invertebrate diversity
Evidence: The characterisation of Ae. albopictus thermal responses has advanced mechanistic efforts to understand temperature-pathogen transmission relationships, however an important caveat to consider is that these relationships are produced under constant laboratory conditions, using a variety of laboratory-adapted mosquito strains [22].
Although we can only speculate as to the relative impact of these factors, our empirical data suggest that at least one key trait, survival, does not differ significantly between land-use types.
325.Rationale: The passage mentions reduced invertebrate diversity in plantations but does not directly link this to reduced predation pressure as the primary factor explaining the abundance difference. The study's empirical data suggest survival does not differ significantly between land-use types, which would be expected if predation were a major driver. The passage also notes that this factor was beyond the scope of the study, indicating it is speculative rather than directly supported by the evidence presented.

325.Option: Greater daily temperature fluctuations in oil palm plantations leading to increased mosquito reproductive output
Evidence: Temperature data indicated that although mean daily temperatures did not differ significantly between land-use types during the sampling period, daily temperature fluctuations were greater in oil palm plantations than in logged forest [55].

Despite warmer temperatures in plantation sites, none of the parameters estimated using the temperature-dependent responses from Mordecai et al. [22] differed significantly between the land-use types for either sampling year (Wilcox, Z = 0.43, p = 0.663; Fig 1).
325.Rationale: The study found greater temperature fluctuations in plantations but notes that these did not result in significant differences in temperature-dependent parameters like transmission probabilities or extrinsic incubation rates. The gonotrophic cycle length, which could be affected by temperature fluctuations, did not differ significantly between land-use types. Therefore, temperature fluctuations alone do not explain the abundance difference.

325.Option: Higher ambient temperatures in oil palm plantations enhancing mosquito development and survival rates
Evidence: Temperature data indicated that although mean daily temperatures did not differ significantly between land-use types during the sampling period, daily temperature fluctuations were greater in oil palm plantations than in logged forest [55].

Despite warmer temperatures in plantation sites, none of the parameters estimated using the temperature-dependent responses from Mordecai et al. [22] differed significantly between the land-use types for either sampling year (Wilcox, Z = 0.43, p = 0.663; Fig 1).
325.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that mean temperatures did not differ significantly between land-use types, and that temperature-dependent parameters like transmission probabilities and extrinsic incubation rates did not differ between sites. The study also found no significant differences in survival estimates between land-use types, contradicting the claim that higher temperatures enhanced survival.

325.Option: Increased availability of artificial breeding sites due to higher human activity and infrastructure investment in oil palm plantations
Evidence: The plantation and forest sites sampled in this study were all characterised by labour-intensive economies, but the level of investment in housing infrastructure and public services was considerably higher in the former. Variation in water storage, waste management and vector control would all have impacted mosquito abundance, but characterising these factors were beyond the scope of this study. Additionally, we did not collect data on human density, however it was likely higher in plantations than in logged forest and may contribute to the higher mosquito density there.
325.Rationale: The passage directly links higher mosquito abundance in plantations to increased human activity and infrastructure investment, which likely create more artificial breeding sites. The study acknowledges that factors like water storage and waste management, which are associated with human infrastructure, would impact mosquito abundance. This explanation is explicitly mentioned as a potential driver of the observed difference in abundance, making it the most directly supported by the evidence presented.

325.Option: Increased availability of natural breeding sites due to forest degradation in logged forest sites
Evidence: The plantation and forest sites sampled in this study were all characterised by labour-intensive economies, but the level of investment in housing infrastructure and public services was considerably higher in the former. Variation in water storage, waste management and vector control would all have impacted mosquito abundance, but characterising these factors were beyond the scope of this study. Additionally, we did not collect data on human density, however it was likely higher in plantations than in logged forest and may contribute to the higher mosquito density there.
325.Rationale: The passage does not mention increased natural breeding sites in logged forests as a factor. Instead, it suggests that human activity and infrastructure in plantations are more likely to create artificial breeding sites. The study does not provide evidence that forest degradation increased natural breeding sites, and the observed higher abundance is attributed to plantation-specific factors rather than forest degradation.

326.Option: The specificity of pyriproxyfen to mosquito species ensures that non-target organisms remain unaffected despite its long-term presence in the environment
Evidence: PPF is not mosquito specific and has been shown to inhibit the development of other insects [13–15] and to impact the physiology of non-target invertebrates [16, 17].
The effects of PPF on pollinators such as honey bees (Apis mellifera) are of particular concern [18–20].
Mass spectrometry data and bioassays confirm the presence of PFF on A. mellifera from cages with PPF treated Ae. albopictus males (Fig 6 and S6 Data).
326.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that PPF is not mosquito-specific and has been shown to affect other insects, including pollinators like honey bees. This directly contradicts the claim that PPF's specificity to mosquitoes ensures non-target organisms remain unaffected.

326.Option: The self-delivery mechanism of autodissemination reduces the need for repeated applications, thereby decreasing the total amount of insecticide released and minimizing ecological risk
Evidence: The intended goal and appeal of autodissemination approaches are that small amounts of highly potent IGRs are delivered to cryptic mosquito breeding sites.

However, while direct targeting of cryptic locations seems like a significant advantage over large scale applications of insecticides, this could actually be detrimental to nontarget organisms by delivering these highly potent long-lasting growth inhibitors to the exact places that other beneficial insects visit, such as locations where many anthophilous insects seek nectar.
326.Rationale: While the self-delivery mechanism does reduce the total amount of insecticide released, the passage highlights that this targeted delivery can paradoxically increase risk to non-target organisms by concentrating the insecticide in areas where beneficial insects are active. The ecological risk is not minimized but potentially increased.

326.Option: The use of male mosquitoes as delivery agents enhances targeted control of breeding sites while reducing the chance of contamination in non-target habitats due to their limited foraging range
Evidence: A. mellifera will travel longer distances to forage compared to Ae. albopictus, so the potential for vectoring PPF across landscapes is high with the observed indirect transfer from nectar sources.
Mass spectrometry data and bioassays confirm the presence of PFF on A. mellifera from cages with PPF treated Ae. albopictus males (Fig 6 and S6 Data).
326.Rationale: The passage indicates that while male mosquitoes have a limited foraging range, the study found that honey bees, which travel longer distances, can become contaminated with PPF and potentially spread it across larger areas. This undermines the claim that using male mosquitoes reduces contamination in non-target habitats.

326.Option: The long environmental persistence of pyriproxyfen enables sustained mosquito control but increases the likelihood of unintended contamination of pollinator habitats
Evidence: PPF has been shown to persist up to four months in the environment and is incredibly potent [8, 9].

The risk of area-wide applications of PPF or other insecticide treatments has not been compared to the risk of using autodissemination approaches. Area-wide spraying of insecticides for insect pests or more specific applications containing an insect growth regulator may be even more harmful to insect pollinator species such as A. mellifera than the amounts of PPF delivered to the environment via an ADAM approach [9, 30, 31].
Mass spectrometry data and bioassays confirm the presence of PFF on A. mellifera from cages with PPF treated Ae. albopictus males (Fig 6 and S6 Data).
326.Rationale: The passage clearly states that PPF's long environmental persistence (up to four months) is a key factor in its sustained mosquito control. However, this same persistence increases the risk of unintended contamination of pollinator habitats, as evidenced by the presence of PPF on honey bees and the potential for bees to spread PPF over longer distances. This dual effect directly addresses the question's focus on factors influencing long-term success and introducing risks to non-target species.

326.Option: The high potency of pyriproxyfen at low concentrations ensures effective mosquito control while minimizing exposure to non-target species through rapid degradation in the environment
Evidence: PPF has been shown to persist up to four months in the environment and is incredibly potent [8, 9].
The concentration required to prevent mosquito development (LC50) is only 0.012 parts per billion [10].
Mass spectrometry data and bioassays confirm the presence of PFF on A. mellifera from cages with PPF treated Ae. albopictus males (Fig 6 and S6 Data).
326.Rationale: The passage states that PPF is highly potent and persists in the environment for up to four months, which contradicts the claim of rapid degradation. The persistence, not rapid degradation, is what allows sustained mosquito control but also increases the risk to non-target species like pollinators.

327.Option: The ability to detect and resolve multiple melting curve peaks allows identification of mixed blood meals, revealing simultaneous host use by vectors
Evidence: Unspecific peaks led to testing mixed feedings, showing that the technique detects both Tm sources. This allows identification of many species from which the insects have been fed, even when there are no standards for those species.

In this case, the melt curve showed two peaks belonging to mouse and chicken amplicons, with their respective Tm. In addition, the dissociation curve had a shape with two falls (Figure S2). These types of curves were analyzed by modifying the normalization area to every fall of the curve to recognize genotypes separately with their respective standard genotype.
327.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the HRM-Cyt b technique can detect multiple melting curve peaks corresponding to different Tm values, which allows identification of mixed blood meals. This capability is directly linked to the ability to infer simultaneous host use by vectors, which is essential for understanding complex transmission cycles where vectors may feed on multiple hosts.

327.Option: The technique's sensitivity to nucleated erythrocytes allows it to detect blood from species with higher DNA content, thereby revealing the most frequent host species in a vector's diet
Evidence: Chicken blood has nucleated erythrocytes, so a large amount of DNA should remain in the intestine for a long time. This could explain the presence of unspecific peaks in the melt curve when insects were fed with human blood because the insects are usually fed with chicken blood in laboratory conditions.
327.Rationale: While the passage mentions that chicken blood has nucleated erythrocytes and thus more DNA, this is presented as an explanation for why chicken blood remains detectable longer, not as a feature enabling inference of transmission complexity. The technique's sensitivity to DNA content does not directly allow researchers to infer complex vector-host interactions beyond simple host identification.

327.Option: The high confidence percentage values generated by the software allow researchers to assign genotypes with certainty, even when multiple host species are present, enabling accurate reconstruction of transmission networks
Evidence: All samples analyzed amplified a 383-bp product... the genotype was recognized with a high confidence percentage (Table 2).

The software then auto-called the genotype and melting temperatures of each amplicon and provided a confidence percentage based on the square root of the correlation coefficient between samples and the reference genotypes.
327.Rationale: The high confidence percentages are useful for identifying specific genotypes when a single host is involved, but the passage does not state that these percentages allow certainty in assigning genotypes when multiple host species are present. In fact, the passage notes that when multiple species are present, the technique detects multiple Tm sources, which requires analysis of the melt curve peaks rather than relying solely on confidence percentages.

327.Option: The use of a single pair of primers for amplification enables detection of a broad range of host species, facilitating the identification of novel or unexpected blood sources in complex transmission settings
Evidence: It is important to highlight that no studies with this number of species standards have been conducted with Chagas disease vectors... the technique used herein only needs a single pair of primers, making it easy to include other species according to every epidemiological scenario with no other standardizations of PCR conditions.
This recognition is possible with a single PCR, making it a quick and inexpensive technique.
327.Rationale: The passage emphasizes that the HRM-Cyt b technique uses a single pair of primers, which allows for the detection of a broad range of host species without the need for species-specific primers. This feature is particularly valuable in complex transmission settings where novel or unexpected blood sources may be involved, as it facilitates the identification of these sources without requiring prior knowledge or additional primer design.

327.Option: The ability to analyze both intestinal content and feces samples ensures that the timing of blood meals can be estimated, allowing researchers to reconstruct the sequence of host use over time
Evidence: The HRM technique proved to work in both types of samples (intestinal and fecal) at least until 30 days after insects were fed. This showed the applicability of the technique even when the blood-meal sources were limited to the triatomines.

Another benefit of feces is the possibility of making a diagnosis of T. cruzi infection and the determination of the blood-meal source; both tests that can be done with a single sample of DNA without sacrificing the insect.
327.Rationale: While the passage mentions that both intestinal content and feces can be analyzed, it does not state that this ability allows estimation of the timing of blood meals or reconstruction of the sequence of host use over time. The technique's applicability to both sample types is highlighted for practical reasons (e.g., non-invasive sampling), not for temporal reconstruction of host use.

328.Option: Increased D-dimer levels after ivermectin treatment suggest that microfilarial killing triggers acute coagulation activation and fibrinolysis
Evidence: The increase in D-dimers observed on D4 post-IVM suggests both enhanced fibrinolysis and coagulation activation which happen simultaneously.

This could be attributed to the close relationship between hemostasis and inflammation, where inflammation induces fibrin formation in the extravascular space, facilitating immune cells functions [20]. Consequently, secondary fibrinolysis generates D-dimers, which correlate with inflammation severity.
328.Rationale: This option describes a biological mechanism but it is specifically related to the acute treatment effects of ivermectin rather than the baseline association between parasitic burden and clinical risk. The question asks for a factor that suggests a biological mechanism independent of acute treatment effects.

328.Option: Elevated gamma-globulins across all microfilarial density groups indicate a systemic immune response that may contribute to chronic inflammation and vascular damage
Evidence: Our study highlighted a polyclonal elevation of gamma-globulins across all MFD groups, suggesting a systemic immune response.

This phenomenon is consistent with a previous study in Ghana, were 75% of participants had gamma-globulins > 16 g/L [17]. This hypergammaglobulinemia might be explained by the influence of genetic as well as environmental factors.
328.Rationale: While elevated gamma-globulins indicate a systemic immune response, the passage does not directly link this to vascular damage or chronic inflammation that would explain increased risk of severe clinical events. The evidence is more suggestive of a general immune response rather than a specific mechanism linking parasitic burden to clinical outcomes.

328.Option: Elevated eosinophil counts in individuals with high microfilarial density suggest a chronic prothrombotic state driven by tissue factor expression
Evidence: Eosinophilia exceeding 5,000 /μL is common among subjects with loiasis. Eosinophils, known to express tissue factor, may trigger blood coagulation [5], leading to thrombosis.

There was a trend towards increased leucocyte, granulocyte and eosinophil counts with increasing MFD (p = 0.011, p = 0.013 and p<0.001, respectively, Cuzick’s tests for trend). Median eosinophil counts were significantly higher in patients with a MFD exceeding 20,000 mf/mL compared to those with no mf (1,016/μL vs 111/μL, p<0.001).
328.Rationale: This option directly links high microfilarial density to elevated eosinophil counts, which are known to express tissue factor and trigger blood coagulation. This provides a clear biological mechanism by which increased parasitic burden could lead to a prothrombotic state and increased risk of severe clinical events, independent of acute treatment effects.

328.Option: The significant increase in eosinophils after ivermectin treatment supports a mechanism of immune-mediated vascular injury due to microfilarial death
Evidence: Post-treatment, the median MFD significantly decreased from 3,605 mf/mL to 1,810 mf/mL. Median eosinophil counts rose from 171/μL to 1,095/μL (p<0.001).
We observed a marked increase in eosinophils, consistent with previous reports of interleukin-5 (IL-5)-driven eosinophilia following microfilarial killing by IVM [19].
328.Rationale: This option describes an effect of ivermectin treatment on eosinophil counts rather than a baseline association between parasitic burden and clinical risk. The question asks for a factor that suggests a biological mechanism independent of acute treatment effects.

328.Option: The association between high microfilarial density and increased platelet counts suggests a prothrombotic state mediated by platelet activation during chronic infection
Evidence: Median platelet counts were also significantly higher in the same group (254,500/μL vs 180,000/μL, p = 0.044).

The notable increase in platelets with increasing MFDs suggests their probable role in filarial infections. Prior research has established that platelets mediate the immunological killing of L. loa mfs [14,15], while data in lymphatic filariasis also demonstrated mfs’ ability to modulate platelet functions [16].
328.Rationale: While the association between high microfilarial density and increased platelet counts is noted, the passage does not provide direct evidence that this leads to a prothrombotic state. The evidence suggests a role for platelets in immune responses but does not establish a clear link to increased risk of severe clinical events independent of acute treatment effects.

329.Option: The use of whole blood versus buffy coat as a sample type may introduce variability in test performance due to differences in parasite load distribution
Evidence: The sensitivity was also high, with the best results obtained with whole blood processed either by the Boil & Spin method (97.62%) or by the QIAgen columns (100%). There were no significant differences between the sensitivities or specificities of LAMP in whole blood vs. buffy coat, being these processed either with the Boil & Spin method (WB and BC) or with the QIAamp DNA Mini Kit (WB QIA and BC QIA), McNemar's p > 0.1.
At low prevalence, the performance of LAMP is similar to the RDT in terms of the numbers of false positives that are detected (Fig 3).
329.Rationale: The study explicitly found no significant differences in LAMP test performance between whole blood and buffy coat samples, regardless of the DNA extraction method used. The McNemar's test p-value was >0.1, indicating no statistically significant difference in sensitivity or specificity between the sample types. Therefore, sample type does not appear to be a critical factor affecting real-world performance.

329.Option: The stability of the kit at 30°C for 12 months may lead to degradation of reagents over time, reducing test sensitivity in prolonged field use
Evidence: The kit has a shelf life of one year if stored between 1 and 30°C, and stability testing has shown that it remains stable for 12 months at 30°C.
It is in a ready-to-use format, with an additional advantage of being based on dried reagents, and therefore does not need a cold chain for transport and storage.
329.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the kit has been stability tested and remains stable for 12 months at 30°C, which is the upper limit of the recommended storage range. This directly contradicts the claim that reagent degradation would occur under these conditions. The study does not provide evidence of reduced sensitivity over time within the stated stability period.

329.Option: The use of a chimeric antigen in the rK28 RDT may lead to cross-reactivity with other parasitic infections, affecting the validity of the LAMP test's reference standard
Evidence: The rK28 RDT included in this study (OnSite Leishmania rK39-Plus, CTK Biotech, Inc., USA) presented a very good diagnostic performance, 98.81% sensitivity and 100% specificity.
Participants who were positive by LNA-M were considered VL cases, while all other subjects were considered non-VL cases.
Lymph node aspirate microscopy (LNA-M) was considered as the reference test of the study.
329.Rationale: The reference standard for the study was lymph node aspirate microscopy (LNA-M), not the rK28 RDT. While the rK28 RDT had high sensitivity and specificity in this study, any potential cross-reactivity would affect the RDT's performance, not the validity of the LAMP test's reference standard. The LAMP test was compared directly to the microscopy reference, not the RDT.

329.Option: The genetic diversity of Leishmania strains across endemic regions may affect the test's sensitivity in real-world settings
Evidence: The kit contains a primer combination targeting two different regions of the Leishmania genome, which makes this test Leishmania genus specific and can be used across the different endemic regions to diagnose all forms of leishmaniasis.

The Loopamp Leishmania Detection Kit is the first LAMP test, available as a kit, which has been evaluated for VL diagnosis. It is in a ready-to-use format, with an additional advantage of being based on dried reagents, and therefore does not need a cold chain for transport and storage.
The kit has a shelf life of one year if stored between 1 and 30°C, and stability testing has shown that it remains stable for 12 months at 30°C.
329.Rationale: The study acknowledges that the test is designed to be Leishmania genus-specific by targeting two genomic regions, but it does not provide evidence of its performance across the full genetic diversity of Leishmania strains found in different endemic regions. Since the test was evaluated in Sudan (where L. donovani is prevalent), its performance in regions with different Leishmania species or strains (e.g., L. infantum or L. chagasi) is not established. This genetic diversity could potentially affect the test's sensitivity in real-world settings outside the study's geographic and epidemiological context.

329.Option: The presence of anti-Leishmania antibodies in patients after treatment may lead to false-positive results in the LAMP test
Evidence: Parasite confirmation by tissue aspirate microscopy is also used for test-of-cure, since serology is useless for this purpose as anti-Leishmania antibodies remain detectable up to several years after cure [1,7].
The rK28 RDT presented higher sensitivity (98.81%) and specificity (100%) than DAT, considering borderline results as either negative (88.10% and 78.22%) or positive (91.67% and 70.30%).
The Loopamp Leishmania Detection Kit had high specificity (99.01%), regardless of the type of sample tested (whole blood or buffy coat) or the sample processing method.
329.Rationale: The LAMP test detects DNA, not antibodies, so the presence of anti-Leishmania antibodies after treatment would not affect its results. The passage explicitly states that serology (which detects antibodies) is not useful for test-of-cure, but the LAMP test is used for parasite confirmation. The high specificity of the LAMP test indicates it is not affected by non-specific antibody responses.

330.Option: The fact that 22 participants were referred for treatment, as this indicates that seropositivity was interpreted as clinically significant regardless of symptom status
Evidence: Twenty-two [22] slaughterhouse workers were evaluated as probable brucellosis cases, prescription was obtained from the nearest government health facility, drugs purchased by the research team and patients referred to the same health facilities for management.

The therapeutic package consisted of intravenous Gentamicin (160mg O.D x 2/52) and Oral Doxycycline Capsules (100mg B.I.D x 3/52). Complete recovery was reported upon completion of the therapeutic regime in 19 of the patients with 3 registering relapses.
330.Rationale: Referral for treatment is a management decision based on clinical suspicion and serological evidence, but it does not directly indicate active infection. The passage states that 22 were referred as 'probable brucellosis cases' but also notes that 'positive results also need to be carefully evaluated because antibodies to Brucella may result from contact without clinical disease and may remain after successful therapy.' The treatment was based on a combination of serology and symptoms, not seropositivity alone.

330.Option: The high seroprevalence of 9.0% among ruminant slaughterhouse workers, as this indicates widespread active transmission in the population
Evidence: The sero-prevalence among ruminant slaughterhouse workers in this study ranged between 7.3% (95% CI: 4.8–10.7) to 9.0% (95% CI: 6.3–12.7) (Table 2). This implies that almost one in every 10 slaughterhouse workers was exposed to Brucella.
Of these, serological evidence shows that at least 1 out 3 (12/31), possibly twice as much (25/31), were actively infected (Table 3).
330.Rationale: While the high seroprevalence suggests exposure, it does not distinguish between active infection and past exposure. The passage explicitly states that 'positive results also need to be carefully evaluated because antibodies to Brucella may result from contact without clinical disease and may remain after successful therapy.' The 9.0% figure includes all seropositive individuals, not just those with active infection.

330.Option: High antibody titers in both RBT and BrucellaCapt combined with self-reported symptoms consistent with acute brucellosis
Evidence: Of the 31 sera positive in the standard RBT protocol, eight had a titre ≥ 1:8 in the modified RBT protocol, a very high titre in BrucellaCapt and seven self-reported symptoms (one case did not return this information) compatible with brucellosis.
The RBT and BrucellaCapt titres and the symptoms in the corresponding persons are presented in Table 3.

The most reported clinical signs in the seropositive individuals were fever, headache, and arthralgia (Fig 2). These were similar to those observed among 101 patients attending Kabale Regional Referral Hospital, South Western Uganda from September 2002 to May 2010 who were diagnosed with a combination of brucellosis and other co-morbidities.
330.Rationale: The combination of high antibody titers in both RBT and BrucellaCapt, along with self-reported symptoms consistent with acute brucellosis, provides the strongest evidence for active or recent infection. The passage specifically notes that 'serological evidence shows that at least 1 out 3 (12/31), possibly twice as much (25/31), were actively infected' based on these criteria. High titers and symptoms together are more indicative of active disease than either factor alone.

330.Option: Seropositivity detected by the RBT alone without confirmation by BrucellaCapt, as this test is highly specific in endemic settings
Evidence: The standard RBT shows excellent performance [21,30,46] and the specificity in persons from endemic areas and risk groups can be optimized by testing serum dilutions.

In previous studies RBT resulted in 87%-100% sensitivity-specificity at a ≥ 1:4 [21] or 74%-100% sensitivity-specificity at ≥1:8 [21,46]. While these cut-offs discriminated contacts with 100% specificity, one study [21] recommended that in cases with limited or lower RBT titers and symptoms compatible with brucellosis, BrucellaCapt should be used as a complementary test.
330.Rationale: The passage states that RBT alone may not be sufficient to confirm active infection, especially in endemic areas. It recommends using BrucellaCapt as a complementary test for cases with lower RBT titers and symptoms. Therefore, RBT alone without confirmation by BrucellaCapt is not the strongest indicator of active infection.

330.Option: The presence of fever, headache, and arthralgia in seropositive individuals, as these are pathognomonic for acute brucellosis and distinguish it from other febrile illnesses
Evidence: The most reported clinical signs in the seropositive individuals were fever, headache, and arthralgia (Fig 2). 330.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that brucellosis lacks specific symptoms and is often confused with other febrile illnesses. 331.Option: Using a fluorescence-based detection system with a 450 nm emission filter allows for real-time monitoring of NADPH consumption and reduces interference from non-fluorescent compounds

332.Option: The absence of other helminth infections in both groups, confirmed by multiple diagnostic techniques, rules out confounding by co-infections
Evidence: Ten volunteers between the ages of 18 and 76 were recruited over the course of two months (Table 1). 333.Option: Use a combination of multiple parasitological methods and antigen detection to establish a composite reference standard
Evidence: The inclusion of additional parasitological methods for schistosome diagnosis resulted in the detection of a much higher number of infected individuals within the study population (Table 3). The apparent prevalence rose from 20.4 to 29.9%, when the number of KK slides was increased from two to 14 slides, or to 38.3%, when we used two slides prepared from each of the three fecal samples. Other parasitological methods that used a higher amount of fecal matter, such as the saline gradient and Helmintex, also detected a higher number of S. mansoni-infected individuals (Table 3). Overall, and taking into consideration the results of all the parasitological methods (reference standard), the prevalence of intestinal schistosomiasis reached 45.9%, which represents a 2.3 times increase in relation to the WHO’s recommended standard KK procedure.

The reference standard was used to evaluate the efficacy of each of the diagnostic methods tested. For the fecal techniques, the best performance was obtained with the modified Helmintex method, which identified schistosome eggs in feces of 88 individuals (40.4% of prevalence). This parasitological method showed a high sensitivity (86.6%) and the highest degree of concordance in relation to the reference standard (kappa = 0.84) (Table 3).

The best KK variant tested herein (six slides prepared from three fecal samples) achieved a sensitivity of 82, 88, and 94% when combined with the saline gradient, POC-CCA or Helmintex methods, respectively.
333.Rationale: The study demonstrates that using a composite reference standard combining multiple parasitological methods (e.g., 18 Kato-Katz slides, saline gradient, and Helmintex) significantly increases the detection of S. mansoni infections compared to the WHO-recommended standard. This approach minimizes misclassification bias by leveraging the strengths of each method to capture a broader range of infections, especially in low-endemicity areas where individual methods may have low sensitivity. The reference standard in this study detected 45.9% prevalence, a 2.3-fold increase over the standard KK method, highlighting the importance of combining methods to reduce false negatives and improve accuracy.

333.Option: Use the POC-CCA rapid urine test as the primary diagnostic tool due to its high sensitivity and ease of use in field settings
Evidence: The POC-CCA identified 108 out of a total of 228 individuals as infected, which resulted in a prevalence of 47.4% and a sensitivity of 64.9%, when compared with the reference standard (18 Kato-Katz slides, saline gradient, and Helmintex). The sensitivity of the POC-CCA was superior to the saline gradient and comparable to the results obtained with six KK slides from three fecal samples. However, the kappa index of the urine test was considerably lower than that obtained with the other parasitological tests (Table 5).
The main shortcoming of the rapid urine test (POC-CCA) was a low concordance with the reference standard, since we found 30 and 35% of false positive and false negative results, respectively.
333.Rationale: While the POC-CCA has higher sensitivity than the standard KK method, it still has significant limitations in low-endemicity areas. The study found that 35% of individuals with confirmed S. mansoni infection were false negatives by POC-CCA, and 30% of uninfected individuals were false positives. This high rate of misclassification makes it unsuitable as a standalone primary diagnostic tool in low-endemicity settings where accurate prevalence estimation is critical. The low concordance (kappa = 0.34) further supports this conclusion.

333.Option: Increase the number of Kato-Katz slides examined from one fecal sample to 14 to improve sensitivity and reduce false negatives
Evidence: The analysis also demonstrated that the sensitivity of the KK method increased from 41.4% with two slides from one fecal sample to up to 66.7% with six slides from three fecal samples. In comparison, if only one fecal sample was processed, the sensitivity remained around 60%, even when the number of examined slides was increased to 12 or 14 (Table 3 and Fig 3).

However, and this goes beyond the already existing data on the evaluation and performance of multiple KK slides, we showed that even using the superior version of the KK technique, which involves analyzing two slides from three different fecal samples, we still missed more than one third of the infected population.
333.Rationale: While increasing the number of KK slides from one fecal sample to 14 improves sensitivity compared to the standard two-slide method, the study shows that this approach still misses over one-third of infected individuals in low-endemicity areas. The sensitivity of 66.7% with six slides from three fecal samples is better than 14 slides from one sample, but neither approach is sufficient to minimize misclassification bias in low-prevalence settings. The study emphasizes that even the best KK variant (six slides from three samples) still misses a significant proportion of infections.

333.Option: Match cases and controls on age and sex to reduce confounding in the estimation of prevalence
Evidence: The study population was of low income and educational level: 90% of adult individuals earned minimum Brazilian wages, and almost 80% had only elementary education or less. The primary drinking water source is the local brook (60% of the residences) and the domestic sewage receives no treatment.

The prevalence profile for intestinal schistosomiasis in different age groups revealed herein matched that from other studies [4, 49,50]. However, if the standard method of two KK slides was compared with our reference standard (18 KK slides + saline gradient + Helmintex), we identified up to 4.7 times higher prevalences in the different age groups.
333.Rationale: Matching on age and sex is a method to reduce confounding in case-control studies, but this question is about minimizing misclassification bias in prevalence estimation. The study does not indicate that age or sex are confounders in the relationship between diagnostic method and true infection status. The primary issue is the low sensitivity of individual diagnostic methods, not confounding by demographic factors. The study found that the prevalence estimates varied significantly depending on the diagnostic method used, not due to age or sex differences.

333.Option: Use a single fecal sample with six Kato-Katz slides and one POC-CCA test to balance sensitivity and operational feasibility
Evidence: The best KK variant tested herein (six slides prepared from three fecal samples) achieved a sensitivity of 82, 88, and 94% when combined with the saline gradient, POC-CCA or Helmintex methods, respectively.

The POC-CCA showed a sensitivity of approximately 65%, which is superior to that obtained with the saline gradient method, comparable with the results obtained with KK variant using six KK slides from three fecal samples, and inferior to the sensitivity found with Helmintex, when the criteria of evaluation were used as indicated by the manufacturer that is, if ‘trace’ was considered a positive result.
The main shortcoming of the rapid urine test (POC-CCA) was a low concordance with the reference standard, since we found 30 and 35% of false positive and false negative results, respectively.
333.Rationale: Using a single fecal sample with six KK slides and POC-CCA would not achieve the sensitivity of the best-performing methods in the study. The study shows that the six-slide KK variant from three fecal samples (not one) achieved higher sensitivity when combined with other methods. Additionally, the POC-CCA has significant false positive and false negative rates, which would introduce misclassification bias. The study emphasizes that even the best methods require multiple fecal samples and a composite reference standard to minimize misclassification in low-endemicity areas.

335.Option: Intermediate levels of flooding agricultural and grassland land cover are associated with optimal environmental conditions for Taenia egg survival, increasing cysticercosis risk independently of direct host contact
Evidence: The percentage of land that was flooding agricultural and grassland had a quadratic relationship with presence of antigen in humans: lower odds of antigen presence were associated with very low or very high percentages of this land cover class, while the odds of antigen presence were highest in areas with intermediate percentages of the land cover class (OR = 1.09 and 0.998; p = 0.03 and 0.03 for the linear and squared terms respectively).

Flooding agricultural land and grassland was also (non-linearly) associated with presence of antigens in humans, indicating a role for landscape factors in cysticercosis. The eggs of Taenia spp. are highly susceptible to desiccation, suggesting this association may be related to varying soil humidity in different landscapes (e.g. soil humidity will be highest under vegetation and in areas that flood periodically) [11]. In addition, human activities vary in different types of landscape, thus, altering contamination and exposure: agricultural land and grassland are accessed more frequently by humans than, for example, woodland, enhancing the possibility of environmental contamination (those working in the field do not always use a latrine) and subsequent exposure to eggs. Flooding may also be related to the movement of eggs, with flood waters potentially resulting in contamination of land with eggs from elsewhere.

Precipitation was also significantly, and negatively, associated with presence of Taenia spp. antigen in humans. Based on the previous discussion regarding flooding and access to ground water, this relationship is not as expected. The southern part of the study area (which is at the lowest elevation) experiences the least rainfall, but includes the largest proportion of flooding land and has a larger proportion of the population using groundwater sources, particularly well water. A possible explanation for the observed relationship is the action of overland water flow following precipitation leading to eggs being washed away, whereas flooding events may be associated with egg deposition.
335.Rationale: The quadratic relationship between flooding agricultural and grassland land cover and cysticercosis risk, with highest risk at intermediate levels, directly indicates that environmental conditions (specifically soil moisture and landscape characteristics) influence transmission independently of direct host contact. The explanation about desiccation sensitivity and soil humidity further supports this as an environmental mechanism. The non-linear pattern suggests an optimal range of environmental conditions for egg survival, which is a clear indicator of environmental influence beyond direct contact.

The southern part of the study area (which is at the lowest elevation) experiences the least rainfall, but includes the largest proportion of flooding land and has a larger proportion of the population using groundwater sources, particularly well water. A possible explanation for the observed relationship is the action of overland water flow following precipitation leading to eggs being washed away, whereas flooding events may be associated with egg deposition.
335.Rationale: The study found a negative association between precipitation and cysticercosis risk, meaning higher rainfall was associated with lower risk. This contradicts the option's claim of increased risk with high rainfall. The explanation provided in the passage suggests that heavy rainfall may wash away eggs, reducing their environmental persistence, while flooding events (which may occur in low rainfall areas) could deposit eggs, increasing risk. This indicates that the relationship is not straightforward and the option is incorrect.

335.Option: Cysticercosis risk is highest in areas with high pig density, indicating that animal reservoirs are the primary determinant of environmental transmission
Evidence: The majority of environmental covariates, when included in a univariable model as non-linear terms, did not significantly improve model fit in comparison to inclusion as linear terms (Tables A and B in S3 File). Thus, subsequent analysis of human data included the percentage of land that was flooding agricultural and grassland as a quadratic term.

The final model for presence of Taenia spp. antigen in pigs (Table 5) indicated that breeding sows had significantly higher odds of antigen presence compared to male pigs (OR = 10.35, p = 0.01), and flooding agricultural land and grassland demonstrated a positive association with the outcome (OR = 1.04, p = 0.004).
335.Rationale: While the study did find an association between pig density and cysticercosis in pigs, this does not directly indicate that animal reservoirs are the primary determinant of environmental transmission. The question specifically asks about factors suggesting environmental conditions influence risk beyond direct contact. The study's focus on land cover and landscape factors as significant predictors supports environmental influences rather than direct animal contact as the primary determinant.

335.Option: Cysticercosis risk increases with the use of unprotected water sources, as this facilitates ingestion of eggs from contaminated soil
Evidence: The use of well water was positively correlated with antigen presence in humans, indicating that contamination of well water with faecal pollutants is common.

Wells, although they may be improved (e.g. covered to prevent surface water influx), are generally at higher risk of contamination as they are often left uncovered or inadequately covered, allowing potentially contaminated surface water to enter. Wells are also shallow in comparison to boreholes, meaning that even when covered, surface water has a shorter duration of soil filtration before entering the well, increasing further the risk of contamination [35,36]. Within the study area, well water is more common in areas with frequent flooding, presumably due to the requirement of a high water table. This combination of flooding and vulnerable water supplies may enhance contamination, thus increasing the risk of infection.
335.Rationale: The study found a positive association between the use of well water (an unprotected water source) and cysticercosis risk. This suggests that environmental contamination of water sources facilitates egg ingestion, which is an environmental transmission pathway beyond direct contact with infected individuals or animals. The explanation about how well water is more vulnerable to contamination supports this as an environmental factor.

335.Option: Higher prevalence of cysticercosis in households with pigs kept in close proximity to human dwellings, suggesting direct transmission through animal contact
Evidence: Movements of pigs and humans have not been considered in this analysis, although these movements are of clear importance for exposure to infection: pigs within this study area have been found to scavenge for food within a mean home range area of 10,343 m2 [41]. The inclusion of land cover within 1 km of the household should partially deal with this limitation.

The proximity to tapeworm carriers has also been identified as an important factor determining the occurrence of cysticercosis [15,16]. As this study was based on a sample of individuals from the study area, information on the locations of all tapeworm carriers was not available and, thus, it was not possible to include this aspect in our analysis.
335.Rationale: The study acknowledges that proximity to tapeworm carriers (including pigs) is an important factor, but this was not included in the analysis due to data limitations. The option describes a direct transmission pathway through animal contact, which is not the focus of the question asking about environmental conditions influencing risk beyond direct contact. The study's findings on land cover and water sources provide stronger evidence for environmental influences.

337.Option: A positive correlation between DHF incidence and vector abundance across all age groups, with the strongest association observed in the oldest age group
Evidence: Univariate analysis revealed that incidence and HI were positively correlated in the youngest age class (Figure 2C); whereas, DHF incidence and HI were negatively correlated in the oldest age class (Figure 2E). Stratification of data according to age revealed a positive association between DHF incidence and HI among the youngest population. In contrast, a negative association was observed in the oldest population.
337.Rationale: The passage explicitly states there is a negative association in the oldest age group, contradicting the claim of a positive correlation across all age groups. The hypothesis about cross-protective immunity does not predict a positive correlation in all age groups.

337.Option: A non-monotonic relationship between DHF incidence and vector abundance in the intermediate age group, with positive correlation in young children and negative correlation in older individuals
Evidence: Univariate analysis revealed that incidence and HI were positively correlated in the youngest age class (Figure 2C); whereas, DHF incidence and HI were negatively correlated in the oldest age class (Figure 2E). 337.Rationale: The passage directly describes a non-monotonic relationship in the intermediate age group, with positive correlation in young children and negative correlation in older individuals. This pattern is consistent with the hypothesis that cross-protective immunity modulates disease severity at high transmission intensities, as the mathematical model predicted and the simulations supported.

337.Option: A monotonic negative relationship between DHF incidence and vector abundance in all age groups, with no variation by age
Evidence: Univariate analysis revealed that incidence and HI were positively correlated in the youngest age class (Figure 2C); whereas, DHF incidence and HI were negatively correlated in the oldest age class (Figure 2E).
